summary: Ronacher argues that centralized hosting services can undermine the distributed nature of DVCS, and he was wary of GitHub-style conversion pressure. He initially dismissed Bitbucket but changed his mind after trying it and seeing a low-pressure community welcome and strong Mercurial support. He positions Bitbucket as a better fit for open source than Google Code because Mercurial enables external contributors without centralized access friction. He praises Bitbucket’s wiki design and data portability while criticizing its weak issue tracker and missing file hosting. He concludes that Bitbucket is a strong hosting option precisely because it lets you leave with all your data, which keeps the service accountable to users.
key_points: Centralized hosting pushes against the philosophy of distributed version control and can be culturally coercive.
For open source, Mercurial’s distributed workflow is a better match than Subversion’s centralized model.
Bitbucket’s wiki-as-repo approach and Creole syntax make local processing and portability easy.
The bug tracker lacks key integrations and import/export, making it the weakest feature.
Portability (ability to leave with all data) is the core trust and accountability mechanism.
money_quotes: Git was designed to not be dependent on a central server and it just doesn't feel right in my eye to force people to switch to a cental hosting platform because it provides things you would be missing otherwise (branchviewers, fork overviews or something).
The big advantage over Google code is obviously that you are using mercurial rather than Subversion.
So I personally think that this alone makes Google code look bad for open source projects.
The bug tracker in bitbucket is probably the weakest part of the system.
I'm really impressed by bitbucket by now and can only recommend it for project hosting.
Most important: you are not selling your soul to anyone.
If you are unhappy with what you get, you can grab *all your data* and go somewhere else thanks to mercurial.
key_insight: The best hosting platform is the one that preserves distributed workflows and data portability, because that keeps power with users and aligns with open source values.
